warpmode.doc: Type of warp (alteration of the waveform) to apply to the wavetable oscillator.  The current options are: none, asym, bendp, bendm, bendmp, sync, quant, fold, pwm, orbit, spin, chaos, primes, binary, brownian, reciprocal, wormhole, logistic, sigmoid, fractal, flip
examples:
s("morgana").bank("wt_digital").seg(8).note("F1").warp("0 0.25 0.5 0.75 1")
  .warpmode("<asym bendp spin logistic sync wormhole brownian>*2")

source.doc: Define a custom webaudio node to use as a sound source.

src.doc: Define a custom webaudio node to use as a sound source.

n.doc: Selects the given index:  - for samples, it picks the sample by index, with wrap around  - for scales, it picks the scale degree  - for voicings, it picks the voice index

i.doc: Selects the given degree. Currently used in `xen` and `tune`:

note.doc: Plays the given note name or midi number. A note name consists of  - a letter (a-g or A-G) - optional accidentals (b or #) - optional (possibly negative) octave number (0-9). Defaults to 3  Examples of valid note names: `c`, `bb`, `Bb`, `f#`, `c3`, `A4`, `Eb2`, `c#5`  You can also use midi numbers instead of note names, where 69 is mapped to A4 440Hz in 12EDO.

velocity.doc: Sets the velocity from 0 to 1. Is multiplied together with gain.

vel.doc: Sets the velocity from 0 to 1. Is multiplied together with gain.

gain.doc: Controls the gain by an exponential amount.

postgain.doc: Gain applied after all effects have been processed.

fmh.doc: Sets the Frequency Modulation Harmonicity Ratio. Controls the timbre of the sound. Whole numbers and simple ratios sound more natural, while decimal numbers and complex ratios sound metallic.  A number may be added afterwards to control the harmonicity of any of the 8 individual FMs (e.g. `fmh2`)
examples:
note("c e g b g e")
.fm(4)
.fmh("<1 2 1.5 1.61>")
._scope()

fmi.doc: Sets the Frequency Modulation of the synth. Controls the modulation index, which defines the brightness of the sound.  A number may be added afterwards to control the modulation index of any of the 8 individual FMs (e.g. `fm3`). Also, FMs may be routed into each other with matrix commands like `fm13`, which would send `fm1` back into `fm3`
examples:
note("c e g b g e")
.fm("<0 1 2 8 32>")
._scope()

fm.doc: Sets the Frequency Modulation of the synth. Controls the modulation index, which defines the brightness of the sound.  A number may be added afterwards to control the modulation index of any of the 8 individual FMs (e.g. `fm3`). Also, FMs may be routed into each other with matrix commands like `fm13`, which would send `fm1` back into `fm3`
examples:
note("c e g b g e")
.fm("<0 1 2 8 32>")
._scope()

fmenv.doc: Ramp type of fm envelope. Exp might be a bit broken..  A number may be added afterwards to control the envelope of any of the 8 individual FMs (e.g. `fmenv4`)
examples:
note("c e g b g e")
.fm(4)
.fmdecay(.2)
.fmsustain(0)
.fmenv("<exp lin>")
._scope()

fme.doc: Ramp type of fm envelope. Exp might be a bit broken..  A number may be added afterwards to control the envelope of any of the 8 individual FMs (e.g. `fmenv4`)
examples:
note("c e g b g e")
.fm(4)
.fmdecay(.2)
.fmsustain(0)
.fmenv("<exp lin>")
._scope()

fmattack.doc: Attack time for the FM envelope: time it takes to reach maximum modulation  A number may be added afterwards to control the attack of the envelope of any of the 8 individual FMs (e.g. `fmatt5`)
examples:
note("c e g b g e")
.fm(4)
.fmattack("<0 .05 .1 .2>")
._scope()

fmatt.doc: Attack time for the FM envelope: time it takes to reach maximum modulation  A number may be added afterwards to control the attack of the envelope of any of the 8 individual FMs (e.g. `fmatt5`)
examples:
note("c e g b g e")
.fm(4)
.fmattack("<0 .05 .1 .2>")
._scope()

fmwave.doc: Waveform of the fm modulator  A number may be added afterwards to control the waveform any of the 8 individual FMs (e.g. `fmwave6`)

fmdecay.doc: Decay time for the FM envelope: seconds until the sustain level is reached after the attack phase.  A number may be added afterwards to control the decay of the envelope of any of the 8 individual FMs (e.g. `fmdec6`)
examples:
note("c e g b g e")
.fm(4)
.fmdecay("<.01 .05 .1 .2>")
.fmsustain(.4)
._scope()

fmdec.doc: Decay time for the FM envelope: seconds until the sustain level is reached after the attack phase.  A number may be added afterwards to control the decay of the envelope of any of the 8 individual FMs (e.g. `fmdec6`)
examples:
note("c e g b g e")
.fm(4)
.fmdecay("<.01 .05 .1 .2>")
.fmsustain(.4)
._scope()

fmsustain.doc: Sustain level for the FM envelope: how much modulation is applied after the decay phase  A number may be added afterwards to control the sustain of the envelope of any of the 8 individual FMs (e.g. `fmsus7`)
examples:
note("c e g b g e")
.fm(4)
.fmdecay(.1)
.fmsustain("<1 .75 .5 0>")
._scope()

fmsus.doc: Sustain level for the FM envelope: how much modulation is applied after the decay phase  A number may be added afterwards to control the sustain of the envelope of any of the 8 individual FMs (e.g. `fmsus7`)
examples:
note("c e g b g e")
.fm(4)
.fmdecay(.1)
.fmsustain("<1 .75 .5 0>")
._scope()

fmrelease.doc: Release time for the FM envelope: how much modulation is applied after the note is released  A number may be added afterwards to control the release of the envelope of any of the 8 individual FMs (e.g. `fmrel8`)

fmrel.doc: Release time for the FM envelope: how much modulation is applied after the note is released  A number may be added afterwards to control the release of the envelope of any of the 8 individual FMs (e.g. `fmrel8`)

bank.doc: Select the sound bank to use. To be used together with `s`. The bank name (+ "_") will be prepended to the value of `s`.
examples: s("bd sd [~ bd] sd").bank('RolandTR909') // = s("RolandTR909_bd RolandTR909_sd")

chorus.doc: mix control for the chorus effect

attack.doc: Amplitude envelope attack time: Specifies how long it takes for the sound to reach its peak value, relative to the onset.

att.doc: Amplitude envelope attack time: Specifies how long it takes for the sound to reach its peak value, relative to the onset.

decay.doc: Amplitude envelope decay time: the time it takes after the attack time to reach the sustain level. Note that the decay is only audible if the sustain value is lower than 1.

dec.doc: Amplitude envelope decay time: the time it takes after the attack time to reach the sustain level. Note that the decay is only audible if the sustain value is lower than 1.

sustain.doc: Amplitude envelope sustain level: The level which is reached after attack / decay, being sustained until the offset.

sus.doc: Amplitude envelope sustain level: The level which is reached after attack / decay, being sustained until the offset.

release.doc: Amplitude envelope release time: The time it takes after the offset to go from sustain level to zero.

rel.doc: Amplitude envelope release time: The time it takes after the offset to go from sustain level to zero.

bpf.doc: Sets the center frequency of the **b**and-**p**ass **f**ilter. When using mininotation, you can also optionally supply the 'bpq' parameter separated by ':'.

bandf.doc: Sets the center frequency of the **b**and-**p**ass **f**ilter. When using mininotation, you can also optionally supply the 'bpq' parameter separated by ':'.

bp.doc: Sets the center frequency of the **b**and-**p**ass **f**ilter. When using mininotation, you can also optionally supply the 'bpq' parameter separated by ':'.

begin.doc: A pattern of numbers from 0 to 1. Skips the beginning of each sample, e.g. `0.25` to cut off the first quarter from each sample.

params.name: amount
params.doc: between 0 and 1, where 1 is the length of the sample

end.doc: The same as .begin, but cuts off the end off each sample.

params.name: length
params.doc: 1 = whole sample, .5 = half sample, .25 = quarter sample etc..

loop.doc: Loops the sample. Note that the tempo of the loop is not synced with the cycle tempo. To change the loop region, use loopBegin / loopEnd.

loopBegin.doc: Begin to loop at a specific point in the sample (inbetween `begin` and `end`). Note that the loop point must be inbetween `begin` and `end`, and before `loopEnd`! Note: Samples starting with wt_ will automatically loop! (wt = wavetable)

params.name: time
params.doc: between 0 and 1, where 1 is the length of the sample

loopb.doc: Begin to loop at a specific point in the sample (inbetween `begin` and `end`). Note that the loop point must be inbetween `begin` and `end`, and before `loopEnd`! Note: Samples starting with wt_ will automatically loop! (wt = wavetable)

params.name: time
params.doc: between 0 and 1, where 1 is the length of the sample

loopEnd.doc: End the looping section at a specific point in the sample (inbetween `begin` and `end`). Note that the loop point must be inbetween `begin` and `end`, and after `loopBegin`!

params.name: time
params.doc: between 0 and 1, where 1 is the length of the sample

loope.doc: End the looping section at a specific point in the sample (inbetween `begin` and `end`). Note that the loop point must be inbetween `begin` and `end`, and after `loopBegin`!

params.name: time
params.doc: between 0 and 1, where 1 is the length of the sample

params.name: depth
params.doc: between 1 (for drastic reduction in bit-depth) to 16 (for barely no reduction).

coarse.doc: Fake-resampling for lowering the sample rate. Caution: This effect seems to only work in chromium based browsers

params.name: factor
params.doc: 1 for original 2 for half, 3 for a third and so on.

examples: note("{f g g c d a a#}%16".sub(17)).s("supersaw").lpenv(8).lpf(150).lpq(.8).ftype('ladder').drive("<.5 4>")

duckorbit.doc: Modulate the amplitude of an orbit to create a "sidechain" like effect.  Can be applied to multiple orbits with the ':' mininotation, e.g. `duckorbit("2:3")`

duck.doc: Modulate the amplitude of an orbit to create a "sidechain" like effect.  Can be applied to multiple orbits with the ':' mininotation, e.g. `duckorbit("2:3")`

duckdepth.doc: The amount of ducking applied to target orbit  Can vary across orbits with the ':' mininotation, e.g. `duckdepth("0.3:0.1")`. Note: this requires first applying the effect to multiple orbits with e.g. `duckorbit("2:3")`.

duckonset.doc: The time required for the ducked signal(s) to reach their lowest volume. Can be used to prevent clicking or for creative rhythmic effects.  Can vary across orbits with the ':' mininotation, e.g. `duckonset("0:0.003")`. Note: this requires first applying the effect to multiple orbits with e.g. `duckorbit("2:3")`.
examples:
// Rhythmic
noise: s("pink").distort("2:1").orbit(4) // used rhythmically with 0.3 onset below
hhat: s("hh*16").orbit(7)
ducker: s("bd*4").bank("tr909").duckorbit("4:7").duckonset("0.3:0.003").duckattack(0.25)

duckons.doc: The time required for the ducked signal(s) to reach their lowest volume. Can be used to prevent clicking or for creative rhythmic effects.  Can vary across orbits with the ':' mininotation, e.g. `duckonset("0:0.003")`. Note: this requires first applying the effect to multiple orbits with e.g. `duckorbit("2:3")`.
examples:
// Rhythmic
noise: s("pink").distort("2:1").orbit(4) // used rhythmically with 0.3 onset below
hhat: s("hh*16").orbit(7)
ducker: s("bd*4").bank("tr909").duckorbit("4:7").duckonset("0.3:0.003").duckattack(0.25)

duckattack.doc: The time required for the ducked signal(s) to return to their normal volume.  Can vary across orbits with the ':' mininotation, e.g. `duckonset("0:0.003")`. Note: this requires first applying the effect to multiple orbits with e.g. `duckorbit("2:3")`.

duckatt.doc: The time required for the ducked signal(s) to return to their normal volume.  Can vary across orbits with the ':' mininotation, e.g. `duckonset("0:0.003")`. Note: this requires first applying the effect to multiple orbits with e.g. `duckorbit("2:3")`.

datt.doc: The time required for the ducked signal(s) to return to their normal volume.  Can vary across orbits with the ':' mininotation, e.g. `duckonset("0:0.003")`. Note: this requires first applying the effect to multiple orbits with e.g. `duckorbit("2:3")`.

params.doc: How much to bend the LFO shape

vib.doc: Applies a vibrato to the frequency of the oscillator.
examples:
// change the modulation depth with ":"
note("a e")
.vib("<.5 1 2 4 8 16>:12")
._scope()

vibrato.doc: Applies a vibrato to the frequency of the oscillator.
examples:
// change the modulation depth with ":"
note("a e")
.vib("<.5 1 2 4 8 16>:12")
._scope()

v.doc: Applies a vibrato to the frequency of the oscillator.
examples:
// change the modulation depth with ":"
note("a e")
.vib("<.5 1 2 4 8 16>:12")
._scope()

noise.doc: Adds pink noise to the mix

vibmod.doc: Sets the vibrato depth in semitones. Only has an effect if `vibrato` | `vib` | `v` is is also set
examples:
// change the vibrato frequency with ":"
note("a e")
.vibmod("<.25 .5 1 2 12>:8")
._scope()

vmod.doc: Sets the vibrato depth in semitones. Only has an effect if `vibrato` | `vib` | `v` is is also set
examples:
// change the vibrato frequency with ":"
note("a e")
.vibmod("<.25 .5 1 2 12>:8")
._scope()

djf.doc: DJ filter, below 0.5 is low pass filter, above is high pass filter.

params.name: cutoff
params.doc: below 0.5 is low pass filter, above is high pass filter

delay.doc: Sets the level of the delay signal.  When using mininotation, you can also optionally add the 'delaytime' and 'delayfeedback' parameter, separated by ':'.

delayfeedback.doc: Sets the level of the signal that is fed back into the delay. Caution: Values >= 1 will result in a signal that gets louder and louder! Don't do it

delayfb.doc: Sets the level of the signal that is fed back into the delay. Caution: Values >= 1 will result in a signal that gets louder and louder! Don't do it

dfb.doc: Sets the level of the signal that is fed back into the delay. Caution: Values >= 1 will result in a signal that gets louder and louder! Don't do it

delayspeed.doc: Sets the time of the delay effect.

params.name: delayspeed
params.doc: controls the pitch of the delay feedback

delayt.doc: Sets the time of the delay effect.

params.name: delayspeed
params.doc: controls the pitch of the delay feedback

dt.doc: Sets the time of the delay effect.

params.name: delayspeed
params.doc: controls the pitch of the delay feedback

delaytime.doc: Sets the time of the delay effect in seconds.

delaysync.doc: Sets the time of the delay effect in cycles.

delays.doc: Sets the time of the delay effect in cycles.

ds.doc: Sets the time of the delay effect in cycles.

detune.doc: Set detune for stacked voices of supported oscillators.
examples: note("d f a a# a d3").fast(2).s("supersaw").detune("<.1 .2 .5 24.1>")

det.doc: Set detune for stacked voices of supported oscillators.
examples: note("d f a a# a d3").fast(2).s("supersaw").detune("<.1 .2 .5 24.1>")

unison.doc: Set number of stacked voices for supported oscillators.
examples: note("d f a a# a d3").fast(2).s("supersaw").unison("<1 2 7>")

spread.doc: Set the stereo pan spread for supported oscillators
examples: note("d f a a# a d3").fast(2).s("supersaw").spread("<0 .3 1>")

fadeTime.doc: Used when using `begin`/`end` or `chop`/`striate` and friends, to change the fade out time of the 'grain' envelope.

fadeOutTime.doc: Used when using `begin`/`end` or `chop`/`striate` and friends, to change the fade out time of the 'grain' envelope.

params.name: frequency
params.doc: in Hz. the audible range is between 20 and 20000 Hz

examples:
note("<c eb g bb> ~")
.release(.5) // to hear the pitch release
.prelease("<0 .1 .25 .5>")

note("<c eb g bb> ~")
.release(.5) // to hear the pitch release
.prelease("<0 .1 .25 .5>")

penv.doc: Amount of pitch envelope. Negative values will flip the envelope. If you don't set other pitch envelope controls, `pattack:.2` will be the default.

pcurve.doc: Curve of envelope. Defaults to linear. exponential is good for kicks

panchor.doc: Sets the range anchor of the envelope: - anchor 0: range = [note, note + penv] - anchor 1: range = [note - penv, note] If you don't set an anchor, the value will default to the psustain value.

label.doc: Sets the displayed text for an event on the pianoroll

octave.doc: Sets the default octave of a synth.

oct.doc: Sets the default octave of a synth.

orbit.doc: An `orbit` is a global parameter context for patterns. Patterns with the same orbit will share the same global effects.

o.doc: An `orbit` is a global parameter context for patterns. Patterns with the same orbit will share the same global effects.

bus.doc: A `bus` is a send which can be used for mixing patterns. It combines with..   s("bus") to play that bus through another pattern (for, say, applying non-linear   effects like distortion to multiple signals)    otherPat.bmod(..) (to modulate another pattern with the bus)

busgain.doc: Postgain multiplier prior to sending the signal to the audio bus.

bgain.doc: Postgain multiplier prior to sending the signal to the audio bus.

params.name: pan
params.doc: between 0 and 1, from left to right (assuming stereo), once round a circle (assuming multichannel)

examples: s("bd rim sd rim bd ~ cp rim").pan(sine.slow(2))

panspan.doc: Controls how much multichannel output is fanned out

params.name: span
params.doc: between -inf and inf, negative is backwards ordering

pansplay.doc: Controls how much multichannel output is spread

params.name: symbols
params.doc: chord symbols to voice e.g., C, Eb, Fm7, G7. The symbols can be defined via addVoicings

examples: chord("<Am C D F Am E Am E>").voicing()

dictionary.doc: Which dictionary to use for the voicings. This falls back to the default dictionary if not provided

params.name: dictionaryName
params.doc: which dictionary (having been defined with `addVoicings`) to use

examples:
addVoicings('house', {
'': ['7 12 16', '0 7 16', '4 7 12'],
'm': ['0 3 7']
})
chord("<Am C D F Am E Am E>")
.dict('house').anchor(66)
.voicing().room(.5)

offset.doc: Sets how the voicing is offset from the anchored position

params.name: shift
params.doc: the amount to shift the voicing up or down

examples: chord("<Am C D F Am E Am E>").offset("<0 1 2 3 4 5>") // alter the voicing each time

registerControl.doc: How many octaves are voicing steps spread apart, defaults to 1   @name octaves  @tags tonal  @param {number | Pattern} count the number of octaves  @example  chord("<Am C D F Am E Am E>").octaves("<2 4>").voicing()

mode.doc: Remove anchor note from the voicing. Useful for melody harmonization

room.doc: Sets the level of reverb.  When using mininotation, you can also optionally add the 'size' parameter, separated by ':'.

roomlp.doc: Reverb lowpass starting frequency (in hertz). When this property is changed, the reverb will be recaculated, so only change this sparsely..

rlp.doc: Reverb lowpass starting frequency (in hertz). When this property is changed, the reverb will be recaculated, so only change this sparsely..

roomdim.doc: Reverb lowpass frequency at -60dB (in hertz). When this property is changed, the reverb will be recaculated, so only change this sparsely..

rdim.doc: Reverb lowpass frequency at -60dB (in hertz). When this property is changed, the reverb will be recaculated, so only change this sparsely..

roomfade.doc: Reverb fade time (in seconds). When this property is changed, the reverb will be recaculated, so only change this sparsely..

rfade.doc: Reverb fade time (in seconds). When this property is changed, the reverb will be recaculated, so only change this sparsely..

iresponse.doc: Sets the sample to use as an impulse response for the reverb.

params.name: sample
params.doc: to use as an impulse response

ir.doc: Sets the sample to use as an impulse response for the reverb.

params.name: sample
params.doc: to use as an impulse response

irspeed.doc: Sets speed of the sample for the impulse response.

irbegin.doc: Sets the beginning of the IR response sample

roomsize.doc: Sets the room size of the reverb, see `room`. When this property is changed, the reverb will be recaculated, so only change this sparsely..

rsize.doc: Sets the room size of the reverb, see `room`. When this property is changed, the reverb will be recaculated, so only change this sparsely..

sz.doc: Sets the room size of the reverb, see `room`. When this property is changed, the reverb will be recaculated, so only change this sparsely..

size.doc: Sets the room size of the reverb, see `room`. When this property is changed, the reverb will be recaculated, so only change this sparsely..

shape.doc: (Deprecated) Wave shaping distortion. WARNING: can suddenly get unpredictably loud. Please use distort instead, which has a more predictable response curve second option in optional array syntax (ex: ".9:.5") applies a postgain to the output

distort.signature: distort(distortion: number | Pattern, volume: number | Pattern, type: number | string | Pattern)
distort.doc: Wave shaping distortion. CAUTION: it can get loud. Second option in optional array syntax (ex: ".9:.5") applies a postgain to the output. Third option sets the waveshaping type. Most useful values are usually between 0 and 10 (depending on source gain). If you are feeling adventurous, you can turn it up to 11 and beyond ;)

dist.signature: dist(distortion: number | Pattern, volume: number | Pattern, type: number | string | Pattern)
dist.doc: Wave shaping distortion. CAUTION: it can get loud. Second option in optional array syntax (ex: ".9:.5") applies a postgain to the output. Third option sets the waveshaping type. Most useful values are usually between 0 and 10 (depending on source gain). If you are feeling adventurous, you can turn it up to 11 and beyond ;)

distorttype.doc: Type of waveshaping distortion to apply.
examples:
s("sine").note("F1*2").release(1)
  .penv(24).pdecay(0.05)
  .distort(rand.range(1, 8))
  .distorttype("<fold chebyshev scurve diode asym sinefold>")

disttype.doc: Type of waveshaping distortion to apply.
examples:
s("sine").note("F1*2").release(1)
  .penv(24).pdecay(0.05)
  .distort(rand.range(1, 8))
  .distorttype("<fold chebyshev scurve diode asym sinefold>")

compressor.doc: Dynamics Compressor. The params are `compressor("threshold:ratio:knee:attack:release")` More info [here](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/DynamicsCompressorNode?retiredLocale=de#instance_properties)

speed.doc: Changes the speed of sample playback, i.e. a cheap way of changing pitch.

params.name: speed
params.doc: -inf to inf, negative numbers play the sample backwards.

stretch.doc: Changes the speed of sample playback, i.e. a cheap way of changing pitch.

params.name: factor
params.doc: -inf to inf, negative numbers play the sample backwards.

vowel.doc: Formant filter to make things sound like vowels.

params.name: vowel
params.doc: You can use a e i o u ae aa oe ue y uh un en an on, corresponding to [a] [e] [i] [o] [u] [æ] [ɑ] [ø] [y] [ɯ] [ʌ] [œ̃] [ɛ̃] [ɑ̃] [ɔ̃]. Aliases: aa = å = ɑ, oe = ø = ö, y = ı, ae = æ.

examples: s("bd sd mt ht bd [~ cp] ht lt").vowel("[a|e|i|o|u]")

clip.doc: Multiplies the duration with the given number. Also cuts samples off at the end if they exceed the duration.

legato.doc: Multiplies the duration with the given number. Also cuts samples off at the end if they exceed the duration.

duration.doc: Sets the duration of the event in cycles. Similar to clip / legato, it also cuts samples off at the end if they exceed the duration.

dur.doc: Sets the duration of the event in cycles. Similar to clip / legato, it also cuts samples off at the end if they exceed the duration.

color.doc: Sets the color of the hap in visualizations like pianoroll or highlighting.

colour.doc: Sets the color of the hap in visualizations like pianoroll or highlighting.

adsr.signature: adsr(time: number | Pattern, time: number | Pattern, gain: number | Pattern, time: number | Pattern)
adsr.doc: ADSR envelope: Combination of Attack, Decay, Sustain, and Release.

midichan.doc: MIDI channel: Sets the MIDI channel for the event.

midiport.doc: MIDI port: Sets the MIDI port for the event.

midicmd.doc: MIDI command: Sends a MIDI command message.

control.signature: control(MIDI: number | Pattern, MIDI: number | Pattern)
control.doc: MIDI control: Sends a MIDI control change message.

ccn.doc: MIDI control number: Sends a MIDI control change message.

ccv.doc: MIDI control value: Sends a MIDI control change message.

nrpnn.doc: MIDI NRPN non-registered parameter number: Sends a MIDI NRPN non-registered parameter number message.

nrpv.doc: MIDI NRPN non-registered parameter value: Sends a MIDI NRPN non-registered parameter value message.

progNum.doc: MIDI program number: Sends a MIDI program change message.

sysex.signature: sysex(id: number | Pattern, data: number | Pattern)
sysex.doc: MIDI sysex: Sends a MIDI sysex message.

sysexid.doc: MIDI sysex ID: Sends a MIDI sysex identifier message.

sysexdata.doc: MIDI sysex data: Sends a MIDI sysex message.

midibend.doc: MIDI pitch bend: Sends a MIDI pitch bend message.

miditouch.doc: MIDI key after touch: Sends a MIDI key after touch message.

oschost.doc: The host to send open sound control messages to. Requires running the OSC bridge.

oscport.doc: The port to send open sound control messages to. Requires running the OSC bridge.

params.name: mapping
params.doc: the control names that are set

scrub.doc: Allows you to scrub an audio file like a tape loop by passing values that represents the position in the audio file in the optional array syntax ex: "0.5:2", the second value controls the speed of playback

lfo.doc: Configures an LFO. Can be called in sequence like pat.lfo(...).lfo(...) to set up multiple LFOs. There are two ways to declare which control will be modulated: 1. Explicitly put `control` in the config (e.g. `lfo({ c: "lpf" })`) 2. If the control parameter is absent, the control _immediately before_ the `lfo` call will be used   (e.g. `s("saw").lpf(500).lfo()` to modulate `lpf`)  Modulators can be referred to by `id` so that they can be updated later e.g. inside a `sometimes`. See example below.

params.name: id
params.doc: ID to use for this modulator

env.doc: Configures an envelope. Can be called in sequence like pat.env(...).env(...) to set up multiple envelopes There are two ways to declare which control will be modulated: 1. Explicitly put `control` in the config (e.g. `env({ c: "lpf" })`) 2. If the control parameter is absent, the control _immediately before_ the `env` call will be used   (e.g. `s("saw").lpf(500).env({ a: 1 })` to modulate `lpf`)  Modulators can be referred to by `id` so that they can be updated later e.g. inside a `sometimes`. See example below.

params.name: id
params.doc: ID to use for this modulator

examples:
s("pulse").lpf(500).env({ a: 1 })
  .env({ c: "s", a: 1 })
  .diode(0.3)
  .sometimes(x => x.env({ a: "0.5" }, 1)) // envelope #1 (0-indexed)

s("pulse").lpf(500).env({ a: 1 }, 'lpf_mod')
  .env({ c: "s", a: 1 })
  .diode(0.3)
  .sometimes(x => x.env({ a: "0.5" }, 'lpf_mod'))

bmod.doc: Modulates with the output from a given `bus`. Can be called in sequence like pat.bmod(...).bmod(...) to set up multiple modulators  Send to an audio bus with `otherPat.bus(..)`.  There are two ways to declare which control will be modulated: 1. Explicitly put `control` in the config (e.g. `bmod({ id: 2, c: "lpf" })`) 2. If the control parameter is absent, the control _immediately before_ the `bmod` call will be used   (e.g. `s("saw").lpf(500).bmod({ id: 2 })` to modulate `lpf`)  Modulators can be referred to by `id` so that they can be updated later e.g. inside a `sometimes`. See example below.

params.name: id
params.doc: ID to use for this modulator

transient.signature: transient(attack: number | Pattern, sustain: number | Pattern)
transient.doc: Transient shaper. Gives independent control over the emphasis on transients and sustains

params.name: sustain
params.doc: Emphasis on the sustains; between -1 (deaccentuate) and 1 (accentuate)

drawLine.doc: Intended for a debugging, drawLine renders the pattern as a string, where each character represents the same time span. Should only be used with single characters as values, otherwise the character slots will be messed up. Character legend:  - "|" cycle separator - "-" hold previous value - "." silence

euclid.doc: Changes the structure of the pattern to form an Euclidean rhythm. Euclidean rhythms are rhythms obtained using the greatest common divisor of two numbers.  They were described in 2004 by Godfried Toussaint, a Canadian computer scientist.  Euclidean rhythms are really useful for computer/algorithmic music because they can describe a large number of rhythms with a couple of numbers.

params.name: steps
params.doc: the number of steps to fill

euclidRot.signature: euclidRot(pulses: number, steps: number, rotation: number)
euclidRot.doc: Like `euclid`, but has an additional parameter for 'rotating' the resulting sequence.

params.name: steps
params.doc: the number of steps to fill

examples:
// A Samba rhythm necklace from Brazil
note("c3").euclidRot(3,16,14)

euclidLegato.signature: euclidLegato(pulses: number, steps: number, rotation, pat)
euclidLegato.doc: Similar to `euclid`, but each pulse is held until the next pulse, so there will be no gaps.

params.name: steps
params.doc: the number of steps to fill

euclidLegatoRot.signature: euclidLegatoRot(pulses: number, steps: number, rotation: number)
euclidLegatoRot.doc: Similar to `euclid`, but each pulse is held until the next pulse, so there will be no gaps, and has an additional parameter for 'rotating' the resulting sequence

params.name: steps
params.doc: the number of steps to fill

euclidish.signature: euclidish(pulses: number, steps: number, groove: number)
euclidish.doc: A 'euclid' variant with an additional parameter that morphs the resulting rhythm from 0 (no morphing) to 1 (completely 'even'). For example `sound("bd").euclidish(3,8,0)` would be the same as `sound("bd").euclid(3,8)`, and `sound("bd").euclidish(3,8,1)` would be the same as `sound("bd bd bd")`. `sound("bd").euclidish(3,8,0.5)` would have a groove somewhere between. Inspired by the work of Malcom Braff.

params.name: steps
params.doc: the number of steps to fill

params.name: groove
params.doc: exists between the extremes of 0 (straight euclidian) and 1 (straight pulse)

eish.signature: eish(pulses: number, steps: number, groove: number)
eish.doc: A 'euclid' variant with an additional parameter that morphs the resulting rhythm from 0 (no morphing) to 1 (completely 'even'). For example `sound("bd").euclidish(3,8,0)` would be the same as `sound("bd").euclid(3,8)`, and `sound("bd").euclidish(3,8,1)` would be the same as `sound("bd bd bd")`. `sound("bd").euclidish(3,8,0.5)` would have a groove somewhere between. Inspired by the work of Malcom Braff.

params.name: steps
params.doc: the number of steps to fill

params.name: groove
params.doc: exists between the extremes of 0 (straight euclidian) and 1 (straight pulse)

clearScope.doc: Clears all user-defined variables and functions from the scope. This removes variables created during block-based evaluation.

withValue.doc: Returns a new pattern, with the function applied to the value of each hap. It has the alias `fmap`.

params.name: func
params.doc: to to apply to the value

fmap.doc: Returns a new pattern, with the function applied to the value of each hap. It has the alias `fmap`.

params.name: func
params.doc: to to apply to the value

filterHaps.doc: Returns a new Pattern, which only returns haps that meet the given test.

params.name: hap_test
params.doc: - a function which returns false for haps to be removed from the pattern

filterValues.doc: As with `filterHaps`, but the function is applied to values inside haps.
examples:
const drums = s("bd sd bd sd")
kick: drums.filterValues((v) => v.s === 'bd').duck(2)
snare: drums.filterValues((v) => v.s === 'sd')
bass: s("saw!4").note("G#1").lpf(80).lpenv(4).orbit(2)

asNumber.doc: Returns a new pattern with all values parsed as numerals.

layer.doc: Layers the result of the given function(s). Like `superimpose`, but without the original pattern:

superimpose.doc: Superimposes the result of the given function(s) on top of the original pattern:

log.doc: Writes the content of the current event to the console (visible in the side menu).

logValues.doc: A simplified version of `log` which writes all "values" (various configurable parameters) within the event to the console (visible in the side menu).

into.doc: Breaks a pattern into pieces according to the structure of a given pattern. True values in the given pattern cause the corresponding subcycle of the source pattern to be looped, and for an (optional) given function to be applied. False values result in the corresponding part of the source pattern to be played unchanged.

arpWith.doc: Selects indices in in stacked notes.

arp.doc: Selects indices in in stacked notes.

set.doc: When called on a pattern `a`, with a input pattern `b` (`a.set(b)`), combines `a` and `b` such that anything defined in `b` and anything defined in `a` that is *not* defined in `b` will be in the resulting pattern.  The structure is maintained from `a`, because the default pattern alignment is `in`, see the section on `Pattern Alignment` in the technical manual in the docs  This is the inverse of `keep`  See examples below

keep.doc: When called on a pattern `a`, with a input pattern `b` (`a.keep(b)`), combines `a` and `b` such that anything defined in `a`, and anything defined in `b` that is *not* defined in `a` will be in the resulting pattern  The structure is maintained from `a`, because the default pattern alignment is `in`, see the section on `Pattern Alignment` in the technical manual in the docs  This is the inverse of `set`  See examples below

add.doc: Assumes a pattern of numbers. Adds the given number to each item in the pattern.

sub.doc: Like add, but the given numbers are subtracted.
examples:
n("0 2 4".sub("<0 1 2 3>")).scale("C4:minor")
// See add for more information.

mul.doc: Multiplies each number by the given factor.

div.doc: Divides each number by the given factor.

struct.doc: Applies the given structure to the pattern:
examples:
note("c,eb,g")
  .struct("x ~ x ~ ~ x ~ x ~ ~ ~ x ~ x ~ ~")
  .slow(2)

mask.doc: Returns silence when mask is 0 or "~"

reset.doc: Resets the pattern to the start of the cycle for each onset of the reset pattern.

restart.doc: Restarts the pattern for each onset of the restart pattern. While reset will only reset the current cycle, restart will start from cycle 0.

setDefaultJoin.doc: Sets the default method of combining events from two patterns (aka [alignment](https://strudel.cc/technical-manual/alignment/)) in Strudel. The default method is 'in', meaning that patterns to the left will (typically) dictate the event timings when combined with patterns to the right. By changing alignment to 'out', the opposite will happen. With 'mix', they will combine their event timings.  Note that we say the _default_ method, because alignments can also be set explicitly with calls like 'add.mix', 'set.squeeze', etc.

params.name: method
params.doc: Default join method to use. Options: 'in', 'out', 'mix', 'squeeze', 'squeezeout', 'reset', 'restart', 'poly'

examples:
setDefaultJoin('mix') // also try 'in', 'out', 'squeeze', etc.
s("saw").vel("1 0.5").note("F A C E").delay("0 0.2 0.3")

gap.doc: Does absolutely nothing, but with a given metrical 'steps'

sequenceP.doc: Takes a list of patterns, and returns a pattern of lists.

stack.doc: The given items are played at the same time at the same length.

polyrhythm.doc: The given items are played at the same time at the same length.

pr.doc: The given items are played at the same time at the same length.

slowcat.doc: Concatenation: combines a list of patterns, switching between them successively, one per cycle.

cat.doc: Concatenation: combines a list of patterns, switching between them successively, one per cycle.

arrange.doc: Allows to arrange multiple patterns together over multiple cycles. Takes a variable number of arrays with two elements specifying the number of cycles and the pattern to use.

seqPLoop.doc: Similarly to `arrange`, allows you to arrange multiple patterns together over multiple cycles. Unlike `arrange`, you specify a start and stop time for each pattern rather than duration, which means that patterns can overlap.

register.signature: register(name: string | string[], func: function, patternify: bool)
register.doc: Registers a new pattern method. The method is added to the Pattern class + the standalone function is returned from register.

params.name: name
params.doc: name of the function, or an array of names to be used as synonyms

params.name: func
params.doc: function with 1 or more params, where last is the current pattern

params.name: patternify
params.doc: defaults to true; if set to false, you will have more control over the arguments to `func` as they will be

round.doc: Assumes a numerical pattern. Returns a new pattern with all values rounded to the nearest integer.

floor.doc: Assumes a numerical pattern. Returns a new pattern with all values set to their mathematical floor. E.g. `3.7` replaced with to `3`, and `-4.2` replaced with `-5`.

ceil.doc: Assumes a numerical pattern. Returns a new pattern with all values set to their mathematical ceiling. E.g. `3.2` replaced with `4`, and `-4.2` replaced with `-4`.

range.doc: Assumes a numerical pattern, containing unipolar values in the range 0 .. 1. Returns a new pattern with values scaled to the given min/max range. Most useful in combination with continuous patterns.

rangex.doc: Assumes a numerical pattern, containing unipolar values in the range 0 .. 1 Returns a new pattern with values scaled to the given min/max range, following an exponential curve.

range2.doc: Assumes a numerical pattern, containing bipolar values in the range -1 .. 1 Returns a new pattern with values scaled to the given min/max range.

ratio.doc: Allows dividing numbers via list notation using ":". Returns a new pattern with just numbers.

fastGap.doc: speeds up a pattern like fast, but rather than it playing multiple times as fast would it instead leaves a gap in the remaining space of the cycle. For example, the following will play the sound pattern "bd sn" only once but compressed into the first half of the cycle, i.e. twice as fast.

fastgap.doc: speeds up a pattern like fast, but rather than it playing multiple times as fast would it instead leaves a gap in the remaining space of the cycle. For example, the following will play the sound pattern "bd sn" only once but compressed into the first half of the cycle, i.e. twice as fast.

focus.doc: Similar to `compress`, but doesn't leave gaps, and the 'focus' can be bigger than a cycle

fast.doc: Speed up a pattern by the given factor. Used by "*" in mini notation.
examples: s("bd hh sd hh").fast(2) // s("[bd hh sd hh]*2")

hurry.doc: Both speeds up the pattern (like 'fast') and the sample playback (like 'speed').

slow.doc: Slow down a pattern over the given number of cycles. Like the "/" operator in mini notation.
examples: s("bd hh sd hh").slow(2) // s("[bd hh sd hh]/2")

sparsity.doc: Slow down a pattern over the given number of cycles. Like the "/" operator in mini notation.
examples: s("bd hh sd hh").slow(2) // s("[bd hh sd hh]/2")

inside.doc: Carries out an operation 'inside' a cycle.

outside.doc: Carries out an operation 'outside' a cycle.

lastOf.doc: Applies the given function every n cycles, starting from the last cycle.

firstOf.doc: Applies the given function every n cycles, starting from the first cycle.

apply.doc: Applies the given function to the pattern. Like layer, but with a single function:

early.doc: Nudge a pattern to start earlier in time. Equivalent of Tidal's <~ operator

params.name: cycles
params.doc: number of cycles to nudge left

late.doc: Nudge a pattern to start later in time. Equivalent of Tidal's ~> operator

params.name: cycles
params.doc: number of cycles to nudge right

zoom.doc: Plays a portion of a pattern, specified by the beginning and end of a time span. The new resulting pattern is played over the time period of the original pattern:
examples:
s("bd*2 hh*3 [sd bd]*2 perc").zoom(0.25, 0.75)
// s("hh*3 [sd bd]*2") // equivalent

bite.doc: Splits a pattern into the given number of slices, and plays them according to a pattern of slice numbers. Similar to `slice`, but slices up patterns rather than sound samples.

linger.doc: Selects the given fraction of the pattern and repeats that part to fill the remainder of the cycle.

segment.doc: Samples the pattern at a rate of n events per cycle. Useful for turning a continuous pattern into a discrete one.

seg.doc: Samples the pattern at a rate of n events per cycle. Useful for turning a continuous pattern into a discrete one.

swingBy.doc: The function `swingBy x n` breaks each cycle into `n` slices, and then delays events in the second half of each slice by the amount `x`, which is relative to the size of the (half) slice. So if `x` is 0 it does nothing, `0.5` delays for half the note duration, and 1 will wrap around to doing nothing again. The end result is a shuffle or swing-like rhythm

invert.doc: Swaps 1s and 0s in a binary pattern.

inv.doc: Swaps 1s and 0s in a binary pattern.

when.doc: Applies the given function whenever the given pattern is in a true state.

off.doc: Superimposes the function result on top of the original pattern, delayed by the given time.

brak.doc: Returns a new pattern where every other cycle is played once, twice as fast, and offset in time by one quarter of a cycle. Creates a kind of breakbeat feel.

rev.doc: Reverse all cycles in a pattern. See also `revv` for reversing a whole pattern.

revv.doc: Reverse a whole pattern. See also `rev` for reversing each cycle.

press.doc: Syncopates a rhythm, by shifting each event halfway into its timespan.

palindrome.doc: Applies `rev` to a pattern every other cycle, so that the pattern alternates between forwards and backwards.

juxBy.doc: Jux with adjustable stereo width. 0 = mono, 1 = full stereo.
examples: s("bd lt [~ ht] mt cp ~ bd hh").juxBy("<0 .5 1>/2", rev)

juxby.doc: Jux with adjustable stereo width. 0 = mono, 1 = full stereo.
examples: s("bd lt [~ ht] mt cp ~ bd hh").juxBy("<0 .5 1>/2", rev)

jux.doc: The jux function creates strange stereo effects, by applying a function to a pattern, but only in the right-hand channel.
examples: s("bd lt [~ ht] mt cp ~ bd hh").jux(rev)
s("bd lt [~ ht] mt cp ~ bd hh").jux(press)
s("bd lt [~ ht] mt cp ~ bd hh").jux(iter(4))

echoWith.signature: echoWith(times: number, time: number, func: function)
echoWith.doc: Superimpose and offset multiple times, applying the given function each time.

params.name: func
params.doc: function to apply, given the pattern and the iteration index

echowith.signature: echowith(times: number, time: number, func: function)
echowith.doc: Superimpose and offset multiple times, applying the given function each time.

params.name: func
params.doc: function to apply, given the pattern and the iteration index

stutWith.signature: stutWith(times: number, time: number, func: function)
stutWith.doc: Superimpose and offset multiple times, applying the given function each time.

params.name: func
params.doc: function to apply, given the pattern and the iteration index

stutwith.signature: stutwith(times: number, time: number, func: function)
stutwith.doc: Superimpose and offset multiple times, applying the given function each time.

params.name: func
params.doc: function to apply, given the pattern and the iteration index

echo.signature: echo(times: number, time: number, feedback: number)
echo.doc: Superimpose and offset multiple times, gradually decreasing the velocity

stut.signature: stut(times: number, feedback: number, time: number)
stut.doc: Deprecated. Like echo, but the last 2 parameters are flipped.

plyWith.doc: The plyWith function repeats each event the given number of times, applying the given function to each event.\n

params.name: func
params.doc: function to apply, given the pattern

plywith.doc: The plyWith function repeats each event the given number of times, applying the given function to each event.\n

params.name: func
params.doc: function to apply, given the pattern

plyForEach.doc: The plyForEach function repeats each event the given number of times, applying the given function to each event. This version of ply uses the iteration index as an argument to the function, similar to echoWith.

params.name: func
params.doc: function to apply, given the pattern and the iteration index

plyforeach.doc: The plyForEach function repeats each event the given number of times, applying the given function to each event. This version of ply uses the iteration index as an argument to the function, similar to echoWith.

params.name: func
params.doc: function to apply, given the pattern and the iteration index

iter.doc: Divides a pattern into a given number of subdivisions, plays the subdivisions in order, but increments the starting subdivision each cycle. The pattern wraps to the first subdivision after the last subdivision is played.

iterBack.doc: Like `iter`, but plays the subdivisions in reverse order. Known as iter' in tidalcycles

iterback.doc: Like `iter`, but plays the subdivisions in reverse order. Known as iter' in tidalcycles

repeatCycles.doc: Repeats each cycle the given number of times.

chunk.doc: Divides a pattern into a given number of parts, then cycles through those parts in turn, applying the given function to each part in turn (one part per cycle).

slowChunk.doc: Divides a pattern into a given number of parts, then cycles through those parts in turn, applying the given function to each part in turn (one part per cycle).

slowchunk.doc: Divides a pattern into a given number of parts, then cycles through those parts in turn, applying the given function to each part in turn (one part per cycle).

chunkBack.doc: Like `chunk`, but cycles through the parts in reverse order. Known as chunk' in tidalcycles

chunkback.doc: Like `chunk`, but cycles through the parts in reverse order. Known as chunk' in tidalcycles

fastChunk.doc: Like `chunk`, but the cycles of the source pattern aren't repeated for each set of chunks.

fastchunk.doc: Like `chunk`, but the cycles of the source pattern aren't repeated for each set of chunks.

chunkInto.doc: Like `chunk`, but the function is applied to a looped subcycle of the source pattern.
examples:
sound("bd sd ht lt bd - cp lt").chunkInto(4, hurry(2))
  .bank("tr909")

chunkinto.doc: Like `chunk`, but the function is applied to a looped subcycle of the source pattern.
examples:
sound("bd sd ht lt bd - cp lt").chunkInto(4, hurry(2))
  .bank("tr909")

chunkBackInto.doc: Like `chunkInto`, but moves backwards through the chunks.
examples:
sound("bd sd ht lt bd - cp lt").chunkInto(4, hurry(2))
  .bank("tr909")

chunkbackinto.doc: Like `chunkInto`, but moves backwards through the chunks.
examples:
sound("bd sd ht lt bd - cp lt").chunkInto(4, hurry(2))
  .bank("tr909")

ribbon.doc: Loops the pattern inside an `offset` for `cycles`. If you think of the entire span of time in cycles as a ribbon, you can cut a single piece and loop it.

params.name: offset
params.doc: start point of loop in cycles

rib.doc: Loops the pattern inside an `offset` for `cycles`. If you think of the entire span of time in cycles as a ribbon, you can cut a single piece and loop it.

params.name: offset
params.doc: start point of loop in cycles

tag.doc: Tags each Hap with an identifier. Good for filtering. The function populates Hap.context.tags (Array).
examples:
s("saw!16").note("F1")
  .lpf(tri.range(40, 80).slow(4)).lpenv(5).lpq(4).lpd(0.15)
  .when(rand.late(0.1).gte(0.5), x => x.transpose("12").tag('altered'))
  .when(rand.late(0.2).gte(0.5), x => x.s("square").tag('altered'))
  .when("<0 1>", x => x.filter((hap) => hap.hasTag('altered')))

filter.doc: Filters haps using the given function

filterWhen.doc: Filters haps by their begin time

within.signature: within(start: number, end: number, func: Function)
within.doc: Use within to apply a function to only a part of a pattern.

params.name: end
params.doc: end within cycle (0 - 1). Must be > start

params.name: func
params.doc: function to be applied to the sub-pattern

pace.doc: *Experimental*  Speeds a pattern up or down, to fit to the given number of steps per cycle.
examples:
sound("bd sd cp").pace(4)
// The same as sound("{bd sd cp}%4") or sound("<bd sd cp>*4")

polymeter.doc: *Experimental*  Aligns the steps of the patterns, creating polymeters. The patterns are repeated until they all fit the cycle. For example, in the below the first pattern is repeated twice, and the second is repeated three times, to fit the lowest common multiple of six steps.
examples:
// The same as note("{c eb g, c2 g2}%6")
polymeter("c eb g", "c2 g2").note()

pm.doc: *Experimental*  Aligns the steps of the patterns, creating polymeters. The patterns are repeated until they all fit the cycle. For example, in the below the first pattern is repeated twice, and the second is repeated three times, to fit the lowest common multiple of six steps.
examples:
// The same as note("{c eb g, c2 g2}%6")
polymeter("c eb g", "c2 g2").note()

stepalt.doc: *Experimental*  Concatenates patterns stepwise, according to an inferred 'steps per cycle'. Similar to `stepcat`, but if an argument is a list, the whole pattern will alternate between the elements in the list.
examples:
stepalt(["bd cp", "mt"], "bd").sound()
// The same as "bd cp bd mt bd".sound()

take.doc: *Experimental*  Takes the given number of steps from a pattern (dropping the rest). A positive number will take steps from the start of a pattern, and a negative number from the end.
examples:
"bd cp ht mt".take("2").sound()
// The same as "bd cp".sound()

"bd cp ht mt".take("1 2 3").sound()
// The same as "bd bd cp bd cp ht".sound()

"bd cp ht mt".take("-1 -2 -3").sound()
// The same as "mt ht mt cp ht mt".sound()

drop.doc: *Experimental*  Drops the given number of steps from a pattern. A positive number will drop steps from the start of a pattern, and a negative number from the end.

extend.doc: *Experimental*  `extend` is similar to `fast` in that it increases its density, but it also increases the step count accordingly. So `stepcat("a b".extend(2), "c d")` would be the same as `"a b a b c d"`, whereas `stepcat("a b".fast(2), "c d")` would be the same as `"[a b] [a b] c d"`.
examples:
stepcat(
  sound("bd bd - cp").extend(2),
  sound("bd - sd -")
).pace(8)

replicate.doc: *Experimental*  `replicate` is similar to `fast` in that it increases its density, but it also increases the step count accordingly. So `stepcat("a b".replicate(2), "c d")` would be the same as `"a b a b c d"`, whereas `stepcat("a b".fast(2), "c d")` would be the same as `"[a b] [a b] c d"`.  TODO: find out how this function differs from extend
examples:
stepcat(
  sound("bd bd - cp").replicate(2),
  sound("bd - sd -")
).pace(8)

expand.doc: *Experimental*  Expands the step size of the pattern by the given factor.

contract.doc: *Experimental*  Contracts the step size of the pattern by the given factor. See also `expand`.

shrink.doc: *Experimental*  Progressively shrinks the pattern by 'n' steps until there's nothing left, or if a second value is given (using mininotation list syntax with `:`), that number of times. A positive number will progressively drop steps from the start of a pattern, and a negative number from the end.

grow.doc: *Experimental*  Progressively grows the pattern by 'n' steps until the full pattern is played, or if a second value is given (using mininotation list syntax with `:`), that number of times. A positive number will progressively grow steps from the start of a pattern, and a negative number from the end.

tour.doc: *Experimental*  Inserts a pattern into a list of patterns. On the first repetition it will be inserted at the end of the list, then moved backwards through the list on successive repetitions. The patterns are added together stepwise, with all repetitions taking place over a single cycle. Using `pace` to set the number of steps per cycle is therefore usually recommended.
examples:
"[c g]".tour("e f", "e f g", "g f e c").note()
   .sound("folkharp")
   .pace(8)

zip.doc: *Experimental*  'zips' together the steps of the provided patterns. This can create a long repetition, taking place over a single, dense cycle. Using `pace` to set the number of steps per cycle is therefore usually recommended.
examples:
zip("e f", "e f g", "g [f e] a f4 c").note()
   .sound("folkharp")
   .pace(8)

chop.doc: Cuts each sample into the given number of parts, allowing you to explore a technique known as 'granular synthesis'. It turns a pattern of samples into a pattern of parts of samples.
examples:
samples({ rhodes: 'https://cdn.freesound.org/previews/132/132051_316502-lq.mp3' })
s("rhodes")
 .chop(4)
 .rev() // reverse order of chops
 .loopAt(2) // fit sample into 2 cycles

striate.doc: Cuts each sample into the given number of parts, triggering progressive portions of each sample at each loop.

loopAt.doc: Makes the sample fit the given number of cycles by changing the speed.

slice.doc: Chops samples into the given number of slices, triggering those slices with a given pattern of slice numbers. Instead of a number, it also accepts a list of numbers from 0 to 1 to slice at specific points.

onTriggerTime.doc: make something happen on event time uses browser timeout which is innacurate for audio tasks

splice.doc: Works the same as slice, but changes the playback speed of each slice to match the duration of its step.

fit.doc: Makes the sample fit its event duration. Good for rhythmical loops like drum breaks. Similar to `loopAt`.

loopAtCps.doc: Makes the sample fit the given number of cycles and cps value, by changing the speed. deprecated: use loopAt or fit instead, together with setCps / setCpm.

xfade.doc: Cross-fades between left and right from 0 to 1: - 0 = (full left, no right) - .5 = (both equal) - 1 = (no left, full right)

beat.doc: creates a structure pattern from divisions of a cycle especially useful for creating rhythms

morph.doc: Takes two binary rhythms represented as lists of 1s and 0s, and a number between 0 and 1 that morphs between them. The two lists should contain the same number of true values.

soft.signature: soft(distortion: number | Pattern, volume: number | Pattern)

hard.signature: hard(distortion: number | Pattern, volume: number | Pattern)

cubic.signature: cubic(distortion: number | Pattern, volume: number | Pattern)

diode.signature: diode(distortion: number | Pattern, volume: number | Pattern)

asym.signature: asym(distortion: number | Pattern, volume: number | Pattern)

fold.signature: fold(distortion: number | Pattern, volume: number | Pattern)

sinefold.signature: sinefold(distortion: number | Pattern, volume: number | Pattern)

chebyshev.signature: chebyshev(distortion: number | Pattern, volume: number | Pattern)

parray.doc: Turns a list of patterns into a single pattern which outputs list-values

partials.doc: Scale the magnitude of the harmonics of one of the core synths ('sine', 'tri', 'saw', ..)  Can also be used to create a new synth via `s('user').partials(...)`

params.name: magnitudes
params.doc: List of [0, 1] magnitudes for partials. 0th entry is the fundamental harmonic (i.e. DC offset is skipped)

phases.doc: Rotates the harmonics of one of the core synths ('sine', 'tri', 'saw', 'user', ..) by a list of phases

params.name: phases
params.doc: List of [0, 1) phases for partials. 0th entry is the fundamental phase (i.e. DC offset is skipped)

FX.doc: Establishes an FX chain. Can be called by chaining .FX(fx1).FX(fx2).. calls and/or in a single .FX(fx1, fx2, ..) call. The fx1, .. are _patterns_ which establish the controls of the given effect. See examples.
examples:
$: s("[sbd <hh [bd | lt | oh]>]*4").dec(.4)
  .FX(
    phaser(0.5).gain(2),
    bpf(800),
    distort(1.3),
    room(0.2),
    delay(0.5).gain(1.25),
    distort(0.3),
  ).fxr(1.7) // sets release time of effects (like delay)

$: s("saw").fm(0.5)
  .delay(0.3) // outer effects are applied *last*
  .FX(coarse(4)) // first coarse
  .FX(lpf(500).lpe(4).lpa(1).lpd(2)) // then lpf
  .FX(distort(1)) // then distort

K.doc: Produces a [Kabelsalat](https://kabel.salat.dev/) modular sound engine. This can be used as either an effect (by including `audioin()` at the beginning of your kabel expression) or as a sound source (via any expression which doesn't start with `audioin()`).  Some helpers you have available to you:   * Strudel mini notation works fine in K(..) via "" or ``   * More complex Strudel expressions (like "0 1 2".fast(4) or irand(24)) can be     written by wrapping them in `S(..)` inside your Kabel code   * We expose Strudel's note frequency under `sFreq` and Strudel's gate     information under `sGate`   * You can use more complex multi-line expressions (like `let x = a; let y = b; x.lpf(y);`)     by wrapping them inside a function in K (see example).
examples:
note("A c e".fast(4)).transpose("<0 2 4 6 8>")
  .scale("F:minor").transpose("12")
  .s("saw")
  .K(
    // audioin().mul(sGate.adsr(0.001, 0.3, 0, 0.2)) // as effect
    saw(saw(sFreq / "2!3 16").mul(8).add(sFreq).lag("0!3 0.1")).mul(0.3) // as source
    .mul(sGate.adsr(0, 0.15, 0.5, "0.1!3 1"))
    .lpf(sGate.adsr(0, 0.2, 0.3, 0.2).mul(1).add(0))
    .add(x => x.delay(S("0.3 0.2".fast(2))).mul(0.7))
    .add(x => x.delay("0.03 [0.08 0.01] 0.01 0.013").mul(0.77)).mul(0.7)
    .add(x => x.delay(.13).mul(0.7))
    .out()
  )

squeeze.doc: Pick from the list of values (or patterns of values) via the index using the given pattern of integers. The selected pattern will be compressed to fit the duration of the selecting event
examples: note(squeeze("<0@2 [1!2] 2>", ["g a", "f g f g" , "g a c d"]))

setcpm.doc: Changes the global tempo to the given cycles per minute

all.doc: Applies a function to all the running patterns. Note that the patterns are groups together into a single `stack` before the function is applied. This is probably what you want, but see `each` for a version that applies the function to each pattern separately. ``` $: sound("bd - cp sd") $: sound("hh*8") all(fast("<2 3>")) ``` ``` $: sound("bd - cp sd") $: sound("hh*8") all(x => x.pianoroll()) ```

saw2.doc: A sawtooth signal between -1 and 1 (like `saw`, but bipolar).

isaw.doc: A sawtooth signal between 1 and 0 (like `saw`, but flipped).

isaw2.doc: A sawtooth signal between 1 and -1 (like `saw2`, but flipped).

sine2.doc: A sine signal between -1 and 1 (like `sine`, but bipolar).

cosine2.doc: A cosine signal between -1 and 1 (like `cosine`, but bipolar).

square2.doc: A square signal between -1 and 1 (like `square`, but bipolar).

tri2.doc: A triangle signal between -1 and 1 (like `tri`, but bipolar).

itri.doc: An inverted triangle signal between 1 and 0 (like `tri`, but flipped).

itri2.doc: An inverted triangle signal between -1 and 1 (like `itri`, but bipolar).

time.doc: A signal representing the cycle time.

mousex.doc: The mouse's x position value ranges from 0 to 1.

mousey.doc: The mouse's y position value ranges from 0 to 1.

useRNG.doc: Sets which random number generator to use. Historically Strudel would use `useRNG('legacy')`, which remains the default. To use a new more statistically precise RNG, try `useRNG('precise')`.

run.doc: A discrete pattern of numbers from 0 to n-1

binary.doc: Creates a binary pattern from a number.

params.name: n
params.doc: - input number to convert to binary

binaryN.doc: Creates a binary pattern from a number, padded to n bits long.

params.name: n
params.doc: - input number to convert to binary

binaryL.doc: Creates a binary list pattern from a number.

params.name: n
params.doc: - input number to convert to binary

binaryNL.doc: Creates a binary list pattern from a number, padded to n bits long.

params.name: n
params.doc: - input number to convert to binary

randL.doc: Creates a list of random numbers of the given length

params.name: n
params.doc: Number of random numbers to sample

shuffle.doc: Slices a pattern into the given number of parts, then plays those parts in random order. Each part will be played exactly once per cycle.

scramble.doc: Slices a pattern into the given number of parts, then plays those parts at random. Similar to `shuffle`, but parts might be played more than once, or not at all, per cycle.

withSeed.doc: Modify a pattern by applying a function to the `randomSeed` control if present

params.name: func
params.doc: Function from seed (or undefined) to seed (or undefined)

seed.doc: Change the seed for random signals. Normally, random signals depend on time, so two patterns at the same time will have the same random values. Specifying a new seed changes the signal output by `rand`. This also affects other functions that use randomness, like `shuffle` and `sometimes`.

params.name: n
params.doc: A new seed. Can be any number.

examples:
$: s("hh*4").degrade();
$: s("bd*4").degrade().seed(1); // Will degrade different events from the hi-hat

rand.doc: A continuous pattern of random numbers, between 0 and 1.

rand2.doc: A continuous pattern of random numbers, between -1 and 1

brandBy.doc: A continuous pattern of 0 or 1 (binary random), with a probability for the value being 1

brand.doc: A continuous pattern of 0 or 1 (binary random)

irand.doc: A continuous pattern of random integers, between 0 and n-1.
examples:
// randomly select scale notes from 0 - 7 (= C to C)
n(irand(8)).struct("x x*2 x x*3").scale("C:minor")

chooseWith.doc: Choose from the list of values (or patterns of values) using the given pattern of numbers, which should be in the range of 0..1

chooseInWith.doc: As with {chooseWith}, but the structure comes from the chosen values, rather than the pattern you're using to choose with.

choose.doc: Chooses randomly from the given list of elements.

choose2.doc: As with choose, but the pattern that this method is called on should be in the range -1 .. 1

chooseCycles.doc: Picks one of the elements at random each cycle.

randcat.doc: Picks one of the elements at random each cycle.

wchoose.doc: Chooses randomly from the given list of elements by giving a probability to each element

wchooseCycles.doc: Picks one of the elements at random each cycle by giving a probability to each element
examples: wchooseCycles(["c c c",5], ["a a a",3], ["f f f",1]).fast(4).note()

// The probability can itself be a pattern
wchooseCycles(["bd(3,8)","<5 0>"], ["hh hh hh",3]).fast(4).s()

wrandcat.doc: Picks one of the elements at random each cycle by giving a probability to each element
examples: wchooseCycles(["c c c",5], ["a a a",3], ["f f f",1]).fast(4).note()

// The probability can itself be a pattern
wchooseCycles(["bd(3,8)","<5 0>"], ["hh hh hh",3]).fast(4).s()

perlin.doc: Generates a continuous pattern of [perlin noise](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perlin_noise), in the range 0..1.

berlin.doc: Generates a continuous pattern of [berlin noise](conceived by Jame Coyne and Jade Rowland as a joke but turned out to be surprisingly cool and useful, like perlin noise but with sawtooth waves), in the range 0..1.

degradeBy.doc: Randomly removes events from the pattern by a given amount. 0 = 0% chance of removal 1 = 100% chance of removal

degrade.doc: Randomly removes 50% of events from the pattern. Shorthand for `.degradeBy(0.5)`

undegradeBy.doc: Inverse of `degradeBy`: Randomly removes events from the pattern by a given amount. 0 = 100% chance of removal 1 = 0% chance of removal Events that would be removed by degradeBy are let through by undegradeBy and vice versa (see second example).

undegrade.doc: Inverse of `degrade`: Randomly removes 50% of events from the pattern. Shorthand for `.undegradeBy(0.5)` Events that would be removed by degrade are let through by undegrade and vice versa (see second example).

sometimesBy.doc: Randomly applies the given function by the given probability. Similar to `someCyclesBy`

sometimes.doc: Applies the given function with a 50% chance

someCyclesBy.doc: Randomly applies the given function by the given probability on a cycle by cycle basis. Similar to `sometimesBy`

whenKey.doc: Do something on a keypress, or array of keypresses [Key name reference](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/UI_Events/Keyboard_event_key_values)

keyDown.doc: returns true when a key or array of keys is held [Key name reference](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/UI_Events/Keyboard_event_key_values)

cyclesPer.doc: A pattern measuring the duration of events, in cycles per event. `cyclesPer` doesn't have structure itself, but takes structure, and therefore event durations, from the pattern that it is combined with. For example `cyclesPer.struct("1 1 [1 1] 1")` would give the same as `"0.25 0.25 [0.125 0.125] 0.25"`. See also its reciprocal, `per`, also known as `perCycle`.
examples:
sound("bd sd [bd bd] sd*4 [- sd] [bd [bd bd]]")
  .note(cyclesPer.add(20))

per.doc: A pattern measuring the 'shortness' of events, or in other words, the duration of pattern events, in events per cycle. `per` doesn't have structure itself, but takes structure, and therefore event durations, from the pattern that it is combined with. For example `per.struct("1 1 [1 1] 1")` would give the same as `"4 4 [8 8] 4"`. See also its reciprocal, `cyclesPer`.

perCycle.doc: A pattern measuring the 'shortness' of events, or in other words, the duration of pattern events, in events per cycle. `per` doesn't have structure itself, but takes structure, and therefore event durations, from the pattern that it is combined with. For example `per.struct("1 1 [1 1] 1")` would give the same as `"4 4 [8 8] 4"`. See also its reciprocal, `cyclesPer`.

perx.doc: Like `per` but measures the shortness of events according to an exponential curve. In particular, where the event duration halves, the returned value increases by one. `perx.struct("1 1 [1 [1 1]] 1")` would therefore be the same as `"3 3 [4 [5 5]] 3"`.

transpose.doc: Change the pitch of each value by the given amount. Expects numbers or note strings as values. The amount can be given as a number of semitones or as a string in interval short notation. If you don't care about enharmonic correctness, just use numbers. Otherwise, pass the interval of the form: ST where S is the degree number and T the type of interval with  - M = major - m = minor - P = perfect - A = augmented - d = diminished  Examples intervals:  - 1P = unison - 3M = major third - 3m = minor third - 4P = perfect fourth - 4A = augmented fourth - 5P = perfect fifth - 5d = diminished fifth

params.name: amount
params.doc: Either number of semitones or interval string.

scaleTranspose.doc: Transposes notes inside the scale by the number of steps. Expected to be called on a Pattern which already has a {@link Pattern#scale}

params.name: offset
params.doc: number of steps inside the scale

scaleTrans.doc: Transposes notes inside the scale by the number of steps. Expected to be called on a Pattern which already has a {@link Pattern#scale}

params.name: offset
params.doc: number of steps inside the scale

strans.doc: Transposes notes inside the scale by the number of steps. Expected to be called on a Pattern which already has a {@link Pattern#scale}

params.name: offset
params.doc: number of steps inside the scale

scale.doc: Turns numbers into notes in the scale (zero indexed) or quantizes notes to a scale.  When describing notes via numbers, note that negative numbers can be used to wrap backwards in the scale as well as sharps or flats to produce notes outside of the scale.  Also sets scale for other scale operations, like {@link Pattern#scaleTranspose}.  A scale consists of a root note (e.g. `c4`, `c`, `f#`, `bb4`) followed by semicolon (':') and then a [scale type](https://github.com/tonaljs/tonal/blob/main/packages/scale-type/data.ts).  The scale name must be written without spaces (because it would be interpreted as a multi-step pattern otherwise). If your scale name includes spaces, replace them with colons.  The root note defaults to octave 3, if no octave number is given.

addVoicings.signature: addVoicings(name: string, dictionary: Object, range: Array)
addVoicings.doc: Adds a new custom voicing dictionary.

params.name: dictionary
params.doc: maps chord symbol to possible voicings

voicings.doc: DEPRECATED: still works, but it is recommended you use .voicing instead (without s). Turns chord symbols into voicings, using the smoothest voice leading possible. Uses [chord-voicings package](https://github.com/felixroos/chord-voicings#chord-voicings).
examples: stack("<C^7 A7 Dm7 G7>".voicings('lefthand'), "<C3 A2 D3 G2>").note()

rootNotes.doc: Maps the chords of the incoming pattern to root notes in the given octave.

voicing.doc: Turns chord symbols into voicings. You can use the following control params:  - `chord`: Note, followed by chord symbol, e.g. C Am G7 Bb^7 - `dict`: voicing dictionary to use, falls back to default dictionary - `anchor`: the note that is used to align the chord - `mode`: how the voicing is aligned to the anchor   - `below`: top note <= anchor   - `duck`: top note <= anchor, anchor excluded   - `above`: bottom note >= anchor - `offset`: whole number that shifts the voicing up or down to the next voicing - `n`: if set, the voicing is played like a scale. Overshooting numbers will be octaved  All of the above controls are optional, except `chord`. If you pass a pattern of strings to voicing, they will be interpreted as chords.

pianoroll.signature: pianoroll(options: Object, cycles: integer, playhead: number, vertical: boolean, labels: boolean, flipTime: boolean, flipValues: boolean, overscan: number, hideNegative: boolean, smear: boolean, fold: boolean, active: string, inactive: string, background: string, playheadColor: string, fill: boolean, fillActive: boolean, stroke: boolean, strokeActive: boolean, hideInactive: boolean, colorizeInactive: boolean, fontFamily: string, minMidi: integer, maxMidi: integer, autorange: boolean)
pianoroll.doc: Visualises a pattern as a scrolling 'pianoroll', displayed in the background of the editor. To show a pianoroll for all running patterns, use `all(pianoroll)`. To have a pianoroll appear below a pattern instead, prefix with `_`, e.g.: `sound("bd sd")._pianoroll()`.

params.name: options
params.doc: Object containing all the optional following parameters as key value pairs:

params.name: cycles
params.doc: number of cycles to be displayed at the same time - defaults to 4

params.name: playhead
params.doc: location of the active notes on the time axis - 0 to 1, defaults to 0.5

params.name: vertical
params.doc: displays the roll vertically - 0 by default

params.name: labels
params.doc: displays labels on individual notes (see the label function) - 0 by default

params.name: flipTime
params.doc: reverse the direction of the roll - 0 by default

params.name: flipValues
params.doc: reverse the relative location of notes on the value axis - 0 by default

params.name: overscan
params.doc: lookup X cycles outside of the cycles window to display notes in advance - 1 by default

params.name: hideNegative
params.doc: hide notes with negative time (before starting playing the pattern) - 0 by default

params.name: smear
params.doc: notes leave a solid trace - 0 by default

params.name: fold
params.doc: notes takes the full value axis width - 0 by default

params.name: active
params.doc: hexadecimal or CSS color of the active notes - defaults to #FFCA28

params.name: inactive
params.doc: hexadecimal or CSS color of the inactive notes - defaults to #7491D2

params.name: background
params.doc: hexadecimal or CSS color of the background - defaults to transparent

params.name: playheadColor
params.doc: hexadecimal or CSS color of the line representing the play head - defaults to white

params.name: fill
params.doc: notes are filled with color (otherwise only the label is displayed) - 0 by default

params.name: fillActive
params.doc: active notes are filled with color - 0 by default

params.name: stroke
params.doc: notes are shown with colored borders - 0 by default

params.name: strokeActive
params.doc: active notes are shown with colored borders - 0 by default

params.name: hideInactive
params.doc: only active notes are shown - 0 by default

params.name: colorizeInactive
params.doc: use note color for inactive notes - 1 by default

params.name: fontFamily
params.doc: define the font used by notes labels - defaults to 'monospace'

params.name: minMidi
params.doc: minimum note value to display on the value axis - defaults to 10

params.name: maxMidi
params.doc: maximum note value to display on the value axis - defaults to 90

params.name: autorange
params.doc: automatically calculate the minMidi and maxMidi parameters - 0 by default

readInputs.doc: Read next web audio block to input buffers

getDuration,getDur.doc: Returns the duration, in seconds, of the given sample. Has optional param `n` (for instance, the `2` in `s("casio:2")`)  Note: `must` be called with await, otherwise you'll get a pending Promise object.

samples.doc: Loads a collection of samples to use with `s`

setMaxPolyphony.doc: Set the max polyphony. If notes are ringing out via `release` then they will start to die out in first-in-first-out order once the max polyphony has been hit

setGainCurve.doc: Apply a function to all gains provided in patterns. Can be used to rescale gain to be quadratic, exponential, etc. rather than linear

params.name: function
params.doc: to apply to all gain values

examples:
setGainCurve((x) => x * x) // quadratic gain
s("bd*4").gain(0.5) // equivalent to 0.25 gain normally

aliasBank.doc: Register an alias for a bank of sounds. Optionally accepts a single argument map of bank aliases. Optionally accepts a single argument string of a path to a JSON file containing bank aliases.

params.name: alias
params.doc: - The alias to use for the bank

soundAlias.doc: Register an alias for a sound.

params.name: alias
params.doc: - The alias to use for the sound

tables.doc: Loads a collection of wavetables to use with `s`

primeArrangementParser.doc: Called by eval-feedback.js right before editor.evaluate fires. Stashes the parser's output so the wrapper can recover human-readable labels.

subscribeRegistry.doc: Subscribe to registry/solo-state changes. Fires immediately with the current snapshot, then on every subsequent emit(). Returns an unsubscribe.

resetRegistry.doc: Reset the registry at the start of each evaluate. Solo state is intentionally NOT reset — it persists across evaluates so the user can iterate on a soloed section without re-toggling after each Cmd+Enter, and so WAV export respects the soloed selection.

setSolo.doc: Toggle the solo for a given arrange/section coordinate. If the same coordinate is already soloed, clears the solo. Any other coordinate replaces the current solo.  Callers are expected to trigger a re-evaluate after this (so the wrapper fires again and substitutes the soloed pattern into the scheduler). The UI does `setSolo(...); editor.evaluate();`.

validateSolo.doc: Check whether the current solo coordinate still points at a valid section in the latest registry. If the user edited the arrange out of existence (or removed the section), the solo is silently cleared and the UI goes back to normal. Called by the arrange-bar after each registry rebuild.

progression.doc: Turn a one-line chord progression into a playable Strudel pattern.  Accepts absolute chord symbols or Roman numerals (when options.key is set). Voice leading, slash chords, and voicing dictionaries are delegated to strudel/tonal. Returns a real Pattern that chains with .slow(), .gain(), .every(), etc. like any other Strudel source.  Use single quotes in pattern files — the transpiler rewrites double-quoted strings to mini-notation, which silently breaks this function.

params.name: chords
params.doc: Space-separated chord symbols or Roman numerals. Per-token modifiers: @N weight (like Strudel's @), *N repeat (like *).

params.name: options
params.doc: sound, dict, rhythm (block/strum/comp/arp-up/arp-down/alberti), bass, style (jazz-comp/pop-pad/lo-fi/folk-strum/piano-bare), key

examples: progression('C G Am F', { style: 'folk-strum', bass: true })
